figures.caption: Fig. 3: Spline-based Object Motion Modeling. To ensure temporally continuous and physically plausible object trajectories, we parameterize the sequence of 3D Gaussians using a Cubic Hermite Spline (CHS).

Fig. 5: Qualitative comparison of reconstructed rendered views. We compare our method (HOIGS) against state-of-the-art baselines on both the HOSNeRF [26] and BEHAVE [3] datasets. We display the full-frame (top) rendering and a zoom-in (bottom) of the red Region of Interest (ROI).
Fig. 1: 4DGS-based and human-centric models fail to render HOI scenes.

Fig. 8: 4DGS-based and human-centric models fail to render HOI scenes on the BEHAVE [3] dataset.
Fig. 7: More qualitative comparison between ground truth and our HOIGS on the HOSNeRF [26] dataset.
Fig. 4: Cross-Attention-based HOI Module. The proposed architecture estimates human-object interactions using human body part features and per-Gaussian object representations.
Fig. 6: Qualitative comparison of reconstructed rendered view results on the ARCTIC [9] dataset.

Fig. 2: Overview of the Proposed Framework. Given an input video sequence, we first extract object-specific information to reconstruct the 3D object shape via a diffusion prior. Based on the reconstructed shape, we initialize 3D Gaussians for each keyframe and use a spline-based deformation as our baseline. Here, time-invariant and time varying HexPlane features are employed for canonical human and interaction modeling, respectively. The final deformation is driven by the HOI module, which integrates HexPlane-derived human features with CHS-based object features to model human-object interactions.
